The sthapati (architect) is the chief of the four classes of builders (sthāpaka). They must be well-versed in all sciences, skilled in establishing buildings, and possess numerous positive personal qualities.

A gnomon is a surveying instrument used to determine the cardinal points by observing its shadow.
For surveying, the gnomon must be set up in the center of a levelled square area on the chosen site.
The best class of gnomon has a length of one cubit, a top diameter of one aṅgula (digit), and a base diameter of five aṅgulas.
The medium class of gnomon has a length of eighteen aṅgulas (digits), with diameters proportionate to its length.
The small class of gnomon has a length of twelve or nine aṅgulas (digits), with diameters proportionate to its length.
For the best gnomon, the base diameter (5 aṅgulas) is five times the top diameter (1 aṅgula).

A circle must be drawn with the gnomon at its center, and the circle's diameter must be exactly twice the length of the gnomon.
The east-west directional line is established by connecting the two points on the surveying circle where the gnomon's shadow falls in the morning and evening.
The north-south axis is the line that bisects the east-west line perpendicularly, derived using a method compared to drawing a fish shape (matsya).
Apacchāyā is a conditional astronomical phenomenon of shadow deviation which does not occur when the sun is in the zodiac signs of Taurus or Virgo.

Apacchāyā is the required adjustment, measured in digits (aṅgula), for the east-west axis (akṣa). Its value, ranging from 0 to 8 digits, is determined by the solar month (zodiac sign) and is applied to the gnomon's shadow to establish the true east-west line.

A fundamental rule states that the chosen height of the Linga must be a whole number of digits, and fractional values must be avoided.
The Nakshatra (asterism) of a Linga is determined by a formula: (Height * 8) modulo 27. The remainder corresponds to one of the 27 asterisms, with Asvayuj being the first.
The Amsa (part) of a Linga is determined by a formula: (Height * 4) modulo 9. The remainder corresponds to one of nine parts, some of which are auspicious and some inauspicious.
The 'thief' (Chora) Amsa is an inauspicious result from the Amsa calculation and should be rejected.
The 'eunuch' (Shandha) Amsa is an inauspicious result from the Amsa calculation and should be rejected.
The 'adversity' (Roga) Amsa is an inauspicious result from the Amsa calculation and should be rejected.
The Vara (day of the week) of a Linga is determined by a formula: (Height * 9) modulo 7. The remainder corresponds to one of the seven days, starting with Sunday.
The 'bloody' days (Krudha Vara), typically Tuesday and Saturday, are considered inauspicious results from the Vara calculation and should be avoided.
For good fortune, a Linga's asterism must not be in opposition to the asterism of the village or town where it is installed.
For good fortune, a Linga's asterism must not be in opposition to the asterism of its sculptor (Shilpi).
The Jati mode of Linga is fashioned from an initial stone block shaped as a regular parallelepiped (square prism).
The Chanda mode of Linga is fashioned from an initial stone block shaped as an octagonal prism.
The Vikalpa mode of Linga is fashioned from an initial stone block shaped as a sixteen-sided prism.
The Abhasa mode of Linga is fashioned from an initial stone block that is cylindrical.
The BrahmaBhaga is the lowest of the three vertical sections of a Linga.
The BrahmaBhaga section of a Linga has a square cross-section.
The height of the BrahmaBhaga is one-third of the total height of the Linga.
The VishnuBhaga is the central of the three vertical sections of a Linga.
The VishnuBhaga is located in the center, directly above the BrahmaBhaga.
The VishnuBhaga section of a Linga has an octagonal cross-section.
The height of the VishnuBhaga is one-third of the total height of the Linga.
The IsaBhaga (also RudraBhaga) is the top, visible part of the three vertical sections of a Linga.
The IsaBhaga is the summit, located directly above the VishnuBhaga.
The IsaBhaga section of a Linga has a circular cross-section.
The height of the IsaBhaga is one-third of the total height of the Linga.
An octagon (Ashtasra) is geometrically constructed from a square (Caturasra) of the prescribed width for the Linga.
A regular sixteen-sided polygon (Sodashasra) is geometrically constructed from an octagon by drawing a tangential circle and marking the midpoints of the arcs.
A perfect circle (Vritta) is obtained from a many-sided polygon (Bahusra) by cutting off the corners.
The Sarvatobhadra is classified as the first type of Linga.
The Vardhamana is classified as the second type of Linga.
The Sivadhika ('where-Siva's-element-is-the-largest') is classified as the third type of Linga.
The Svastika is classified as the fourth type of Linga.
The Sarvatobhadra type of Linga is considered most fitting and brings good fortune to Brahmins.
The Vardhamana type of Linga is considered to promote the increase and prosperity of kings (Kshatriyas).
The Sivadhika type of Linga is considered to bring wealth to Vaisyas.
The Svastika type of Linga is considered to bring luck to Shudras (the fourth class).
The lower part (adhobhaga) is a component of the Sarvatobhadra Linga.
The median part (madhyabhaga) is a component of the Sarvatobhadra Linga.
The top, worshipped part (pujabhaga) is a component of the Sarvatobhadra Linga.
The height of the lower part of a Sarvatobhadra Linga is defined as one-third (10/30) of the total Linga height.
The height of the median part of a Sarvatobhadra Linga is defined as one-third (10/30) of the total Linga height.
The height of the top part of a Sarvatobhadra Linga is defined as one-third (10/30) of the total Linga height.
The top part of the Linga is referred to as Shambhu's element.
The Sarvatobhadra Linga is specified as being invariably suitable for the brahmin class.
The Vardhamana Linga is prescribed for princes (ksatriya), bringing them escalating success, victory, and an increase in sons.
A Vardhamana Linga can have its height divided into 15 parts, with proportions of 4 (Brahma), 5 (Visnu), and 6 (Siva).
In the first variety of Vardhamana Linga, the Brahma element's height is 4/15 of the total height.
In the first variety of Vardhamana Linga, the Visnu element's height is 5/15 of the total height.
In the first variety of Vardhamana Linga, the Siva element's height is 6/15 of the total height.
A Vardhamana Linga can have its height divided into 18 parts, with proportions of 5 (Brahma), 6 (Visnu), and 7 (Siva).
In the second variety of Vardhamana Linga, the Brahma element's height is 5/18 of the total height.
In the second variety of Vardhamana Linga, the Visnu element's height is 6/18 of the total height.
In the second variety of Vardhamana Linga, the Siva element's height is 7/18 of the total height.
The Sivadhika Linga, where Siva's element is the largest, is prescribed for vaisya, bringing them all sorts of riches.
The text refers to Brahma's element as the element of Aja.
A Sivadhika Linga can have its height divided into 22 parts, with proportions of 7 (Aja/Brahma), 7 (Visnu), and 8 (Siva).
In the first variety of Sivadhika Linga, the Aja (Brahma) element's height is 7/22 of the total height.
In the first variety of Sivadhika Linga, the Visnu element's height is 7/22 of the total height.
In the first variety of Sivadhika Linga, the Siva element's height is 8/22 of the total height.
The Svastika Linga is prescribed for the Sudra class, and it is said to fulfil their desires.
The height of the bottom element of a Svastika Linga is two-ninths of the total Linga height.
The height of the median element of a Svastika Linga is three-ninths of the total Linga height.
The height of the worshipped element of a Svastika Linga is four-ninths of the total Linga height.
The Surarcita Linga is suitable for everybody (all classes) and brings the satisfaction of all desires.
The Dharalinga is suitable for everybody (all classes) and brings the satisfaction of all desires.
The Sahasralinga is suitable for everybody (all classes) and brings the satisfaction of all desires.
The Trairasika Linga is suitable for everybody (all classes) and brings the satisfaction of all desires.
A Surarcita Linga is defined by its width being equal to one-quarter of its height.
The Dharalinga is a type of faceted Linga.
The upper part of a Dharalinga has twice the number of faces as its bottom part. The bottom part may have 4, 8, or 16 faces.
A Sahasralinga is created from a Sarvatobhadra Linga by cutting 25 facets on its worshipped element and drawing 40 superposed Lingas on each facet, resulting in 1000 Lingas.
The circumference of the circular element of a Trairasika Linga is six-ninths of the Linga's total height.
The perimeter of the octagonal element of a Trairasika Linga is seven-ninths of the Linga's total height.
The perimeter of the square base of a Trairasika Linga is eight-ninths of the Linga's total height.
The height of the Aja (Brahma) element of a Trairasika Linga is three-ninths of the total height.
The height of the Hari (Visnu) element of a Trairasika Linga is three-ninths of the total height.
The height of the Hara (Siva) element of a Trairasika Linga is three-ninths of the total height.
The text refers to Visnu's element as the element of Hari.
The text refers to Siva's element as the element of Hara.
There are four varieties of Linga fitting for Rsis, defined by their shape.
A variety of Rsi Linga where the bottom element is wider than the other two elements.
A variety of Rsi Linga where the median element is convex, like the middle of a barley grain.
A variety of Rsi Linga where the median element is concave, like the middle of an ant.
A variety of Rsi Linga where the width of the bottom, middle, or top is decreased by one-eighth of the chosen width.
A Svayambhu Linga is a self-generated type of Linga, which can be naturally shaped like a plank. Its original form should not be rectified or amended.
When a Linga is installed, the lower boundary of its Pujabhaga (the honored, visible element) must be positioned at a level lower than the upper face of the Pitha (pedestal).
The rounded top of a Linga (Linga_mastaka_vartula) can have a profile shaped like a parasol (chatrakara).
The rounded top of a Linga (Linga_mastaka_vartula) can have a profile shaped like a cucumber (trapusakara).
The rounded top of a Linga (Linga_mastaka_vartula) can have a profile shaped like a hen's egg (kukkutandakara).
The rounded top of a Linga (Linga_mastaka_vartula) can have a profile shaped like a hemisphere (ardhacandrakara).
The rounded top of a Linga (Linga_mastaka_vartula) can have a profile shaped like a bubble (budbudakara).
The height of a parasol-shaped (chatrakara) rounded top can be 1/16th of the Linga's width.
The height of a parasol-shaped (chatrakara) rounded top can be 2/16ths of the Linga's width.
The height of a parasol-shaped (chatrakara) rounded top can be 3/16ths of the Linga's width.
The height of a parasol-shaped (chatrakara) rounded top can be 4/16ths of the Linga's width.
The height of a cucumber-shaped (trapusakara) rounded top is 2.5/6ths of the Linga's width.
The height of a hen's egg-shaped (kukkutandakara) rounded top is one-half of the Linga's width.
The height of a hemispheric (ardhacandrakara) rounded top is one-third of the Linga's width.
The height of a bubble-shaped (budbudakara) rounded top is 3.5/8ths of the Linga's width.
A general proportion for all Linga types states that the height of the rounded part of the head is one-third the height of the upper element (Pujabhaga).
For a Linga with a small head, the height of its rounded part is 1/36th of the total height of the Linga.
For a Linga with a medium head, the height of its rounded part is 2/36ths of the total height of the Linga.
For a Linga with a large head, the height of its rounded part is 3/36ths of the total height of the Linga.
During the installation ceremony, the Linga is placed within a pavilion (maṇḍapa).
The pavilion (maṇḍapa) for the Linga installation ceremony is situated near the main temple (prāsāda).
The Linga is installed upon a couch (śayana) during the ceremony.
The ceremonial couch (śayana) is arranged on a stand (pīṭha).
The stand (pīṭha) for the Linga is placed upon a spread of rice, paddy, and other grains (dhānya).
The ajasūtra, or 'line of Aja', is a primary line marked on the surface of the Linga.
The pārśvarekhā, or lateral lines, are marked on the surface of the Linga as part of its features.
The pārsvaśūla, or frenum, is a sculpted feature outlined on the Linga.
The śivāṁśa is the 'element of Siva', which constitutes the primary vertical section of the Linga used for proportional measurements.
The nāgara Linga is a specific typological category of Linga.
The drāviḍa Linga is a specific typological category of Linga.
For a small (kṣudra) nāgara Linga, the height of the frenum (pārsvaśūla) is 7/16th of the total height of the śivāṁśa.
For a medium (madhyama) nāgara Linga, the height of the frenum (pārsvaśūla) is 8/16th (or 1/2) of the total height of the śivāṁśa.
For a large (jyeṣṭha) nāgara Linga, the height of the frenum (pārsvaśūla) is 9/16th of the total height of the śivāṁśa.
For a nāgara Linga, the width of the frenum (pārsvaśūla) is two-thirds of one part (aṁśa), where the śivāṁśa height is 16 parts. This equates to a ratio of (2/3)/16 or ~1/24th of the śivāṁśa height.
For a small (kṣudra) drāviḍa Linga, the height of the frenum (pārsvaśūla) is 9/15th of the total height of the śivāṁśa.
For a medium (madhyama) drāviḍa Linga, the height of the frenum (pārsvaśūla) is 10/15th (or 2/3) of the total height of the śivāṁśa.
For a large (jyeṣṭha) drāviḍa Linga, the height of the frenum (pārsvaśūla) is 11/15th of the total height of the śivāṁśa.
For a drāviḍa Linga, the width of the frenum (pārsvaśūla) is half of one part (aṁśa), where the śivāṁśa height is 15 parts. This equates to a ratio of (1/2)/15 or 1/30th of the śivāṁśa height.
The largest sized Vesara Linga (Uttama) is a specific classification within the Vesara Linga type.
The medium sized Vesara Linga (Madhyama) is a specific classification within the Vesara Linga type.
The small sized Vesara Linga (Kanyasa) is a specific classification within the Vesara Linga type.
For the largest Vesara Linga, the height of the frenum (Brahmasutra) is 10/15ths of the height of the Siva element (SivaBhaga).
For the largest Vesara Linga, the width of the frenum (Brahmasutra) is 1/16th of the diameter of the Linga.
For the largest Vesara Linga, the lateral lines (ParsvaSutra) start from a point located at 8/10ths of the height of the frenum (Brahmasutra).
For a medium Vesara Linga, the height of the frenum (Brahmasutra) is 4/8ths (or 1/2) of the height of the Siva element.
For a medium Vesara Linga, the width of the frenum (Brahmasutra) is two-thirds of one-eighth (i.e., 1/12th) of the height of the Siva element.
For a medium Vesara Linga, the lateral lines (ParsvaSutra) start from a point situated at 3/4ths of the height of the frenum (Brahmasutra).
For a small Vesara Linga, the width of the frenum (Brahmasutra) is 2/12ths (or 1/6th) of the height of the Siva element.
On all types of Linga, the depth of the lines (Sutra) must be equal to their width.
On all types of Linga, the width of the lines (Sutra) is equal to 1/9th of the chosen fractional unit (Amsa) of the Siva element.
For a one-cubit (EkaHasta) Linga, the width and depth of its lines (Sutra) are specified as 8/9ths of a barley grain (Yava).
For a nine-cubit (NavaHasta) Linga, the width and depth of its lines (Sutra) are specified as 8 barley grains (Yava).
As an alternative rule for a one-cubit (EkaHasta) Linga, the width and depth of its lines (Sutra) can be half a barley grain (Yava).
For the largest Linga, the width and depth of its lines (Sutra) are four and a half barley grains (Yava).
The width of the lateral lines (ParsvaSutra) is half the width of the median lines (MadhyaSutra).
For Nagara and other Linga types, the combined height of the frenum and its bud (Brahmasutra and Mukula) is 10/16ths of the venerated part's (SivaBhaga) height.
The frenum and bud are located in the central portion of the venerated part (SivaBhaga), positioned above the lower 2/16ths and below the upper 4/16ths of its height.
The width of the bud (Mukula) is equal to 1/16th of the height of the venerated part (SivaBhaga).
As a rule fitting for all Lingas, the height of the urethra (Mutramarga) is 10/16ths of the Siva element's height.
The urethra (Mutramarga) is located in the section of the Siva element that is above the bottom 2/16ths of its total height.
In one variation, the height of the frenum (ajasūtra) and its bud is defined as nine-twelfths (75%) of the height of the Rudra element of the Linga.
In an alternative scheme, the height of the Aja line (ajasūtra) is eleven-eighteenths (~61.1%) of the height of the Siva element of the Linga.
The bud at the top of the Aja line (ajasūtra) should have a height equal to one-eleventh (~9.1%) of the total height of the Aja line itself.
In another variation, the height of the frenum (ajasūtra) is ten-sixteenths (62.5%) of the height of the Siva element of the Linga.
The lateral lines on the frenum (ajasūtra) originate at a point located at eight-tenths (80%) of its total height from the bottom.
The width of the frenum (ajasūtra) is prescribed to be equal to its height, establishing a 1:1 aspect ratio.

The shape of the frenum's (ajasūtra) top is conditionally dependent on the Linga's summit type. For instance, a Linga with a parasol-shaped top requires a frenum top shaped like an elephant’s eye (akṣi).
For any Linga, the top of the frenum (ajasūtra) can adopt one of three common rounded shapes: that of an asvattha leaf, a kadali bud, or an ambuja bud.
The width of the median frenum (ajasūtra) is specified as two-fifty-firsts (~3.92%) of the Linga's total diameter.
The width of the lateral lines is defined as half (50%) the width of the median frenum (ajasūtra) they accompany.
Nāgara is a classification or type within the broader category of Linga, with its own corresponding rules for dimensions and features.
A Linga is classified as 'small-sized' when the height of its venerated portion (pūjābhāga) measures between one and six digits (aṅgula).
A Linga is classified as 'medium-sized' when the height of its venerated portion (pūjābhāga) measures between seven and twelve digits (aṅgula).
A Linga is classified as 'large-sized' when the height of its venerated portion (pūjābhāga) measures between thirteen and eighteen digits (aṅgula).
An alternative measurement system allows for the height of a Linga's venerated part to be defined using increments of a half-digit (aṅgula), expanding the number of possible heights.
The height of the portion of a Linga sunk into its socle (Pitha) is defined as one-half or one-third the height of its venerated element (Pujabhaga).
The width of a Linga's venerated element (Pujabhaga) is defined as being equal to, three-quarters of, or one-half of its own height.
A frenum (Brahmasutra) is a prescribed feature that can be drawn on a Linga.
The width of the pedestal (Pitha) is defined as two, two and a half, or three times the width of the Linga it supports.
A rim (Mekhala) is a component of the pedestal (Pitha).
A spout (Nala) is a component arranged on the pedestal's rim.
The height of the pedestal's rim (Mekhala) is defined as being equal to or three-quarters of the height of the Linga's venerated element (Pujabhaga).
A 'Manilinga' is defined as any Linga that is not made of stone.
A Linga made of earth (MrinmayaLinga) is a type of Manilinga.
A Linga made of wood (DarumayaLinga) is a type of Manilinga.
A Linga made of precious stones (RatnajaLinga) is a type of Manilinga.
A Linga made of metal (LohajaLinga) is a type of Manilinga.
A Linga made of precious stones must be installed on a metal pedestal.
A Banalinga is a specific type of Linga.
The height of the visible, venerated element of a Banalinga is defined as three-fifths, one-half, or two-thirds of the Banalinga's total height.
A small-sized Linga (LaghimaLinga) is to be installed when its temple (Prasada) is completely finished.
A medium-sized Linga (MadhyamaLinga) is to be installed when its temple (Prasada) is half-built.
A large-sized Linga (JyesthaLinga) is to be installed once the temple's base (Adhisthana) has been constructed.
The base (Adhisthana) is a foundational part of the temple (Prasada).
As an exception to material matching, a stone Linga (SailajaLinga) may be installed on a baked brick pedestal (IstakaPitha).
As an exception to material matching, a wooden Linga (DarumayaLinga) may be installed on a baked brick pedestal (IstakaPitha).
A metal Linga (LohajaLinga) must be installed on a metal pedestal (LohajaPitha).
The minimum width for a pedestal (Pitha) is defined as twice the height of the Linga's venerated element (Pujabhaga).
The maximum width for a pedestal (Pitha) is defined as being equal to the total height of the Linga.
The minimum width of a Pitha (pedestal) is defined as half the height of the Linga it supports.
The maximum width of a Pitha (pedestal) is defined as three-quarters of the height of the Linga it supports.
An alternative rule states the width of a Pitha (pedestal) can be three times the diameter of the Linga.
An alternative rule states the width of a Pitha (pedestal) can be equal to the circumference of the Linga.
An alternative rule states the width of a Pitha (pedestal) can be one-third of the circumference of the Linga.
The width of a Pitha (pedestal) at its summit is decreased by one-eighth relative to its base width.
The width at the bottom of a Pitha (pedestal) corresponds to the width of the borders of its plinth.
The width at the top of a Pitha (pedestal) corresponds to the width of its upper string-course.
The height of a Pitha (pedestal) can be equal to the height of the Visnu element (Visnubhaga) of the Linga.
The height of a Pitha (pedestal) can be one and a quarter times the height of the Visnu element (Visnubhaga) of the Linga.
The height of a Pitha (pedestal) can be one and a half times the height of the Visnu element (Visnubhaga) of the Linga.
A Pitha (pedestal) can take one of fourteen geometric forms, including polygonal (4, 6, 8, 12, 16 sides), circular, triangular, semicircular, and elongated versions of these.
Regularly shaped pedestals (e.g., square, circular, octagonal) are the appropriate base for a Linga.
Elongated pedestals (Ayata Pitha), which serve as 'thrones', are appropriate for images (murti).
Triangular pedestals (Trikona Pitha) are appropriate for symbolic idols.
Semicircular pedestals (Ardhacandra Pitha) are appropriate for iconic idols.

Padmapitha is one of the nine principal types of pedestal (Pitha).

The Vajrapadma is a type of pedestal (pitha), suitable for all Lingas, with its total height divided into 14 parts.
